en.extract:
The long-tailed tit or long-tailed bushtit (Aegithalos caudatus) is a common bird species belonging to the bushtit family found throughout Eurasia. An insectivore, it inhabits deciduous and mixed woodlands as well as scrub, heathland, farmland, parks and gardens. The plumage of this small long-tailed bird is predominantly black and white with varying amounts of pink and grey. Northern subspecies are paler and have completely white heads, lacking the large dark eyebrows of southern populations. It is a social bird that forms compact family flocks of six to seventeen individuals outside of the breeding season, when these flocks break up. It has a strong preference for nesting in scrub areas and often builds its nest in thorny bushes less than 3 metres (10 feet) above the ground.


Taxonomy and systematics
The long-tailed tit was formally described by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in 1758 in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae under the binomial name Parus caudatus. The current genus name Aegithalos was a term used by Aristotle for some European tits, including the long-tailed tit. The specific epithet caudatus is the Latin word for "tailed". Linnaeus did not invent this Latin name. "Parus caudatus" had been used by earlier authors such as the Swiss naturalist Conrad Gessner in 1555, the Italian naturalist Ulisse Aldrovandi in 1599, and the English ornithologist Francis Willughby in 1676. Willughby listed the English name as the "long tail'd titmouse". Its previous common nickname in everyday English was the bum-towel, from the shape of its tail.
The long-tailed tit was originally classified as a true tit of the Parus genus. Since then, it and its relatives have  been reclassified as a distinct family, the Aegithalidae, containing three genera:

Aegithalos (long-tailed tits), nine living species including A. caudatus
Psaltria (pygmy bushtit), monotypic
Psaltriparus (American bushtit), monotypic.
Aegithalos caudatus caudatus × europaeus (Northern × Central European Long-tailed Tit)

This species is the only representative of the family Aegithalidae found in northern Eurasia. The long-tailed tit exhibits complex global variation with 17 races recognised, divisible into three groups:

the A. c. caudatus group in northern Europe and Asia. A. c. caudatus has a pure white head
the A. c. europaeus group in southern and western Europe, north-east China, and Japan. Separating A. c. rosaceus from other members of the A. c. europaeus group though is problematic, relying on varying thickness of the crown stripes and amount of streaks and colour on the underparts
the A. c. alpinus group in Mediterranean Europe and south-west Asia.
The silver-throated bushtit (Aegithalos glaucogularis) from eastern China was formerly considered conspecific with the long-tailed tit, but was later split due to significant plumage and genetic differences.
Where the groups meet, there are extensive areas occupied by highly variable 'hybrids'. The British long-tailed tit, subspecies A. c. rosaceus, belongs to the A. c. europaeus group.


Description
This species has been described as a tiny (at only 13–15 cm (5–6 in) in length, including its 7–9 cm (3–3+1⁄2 in) tail), round-bodied tit with a short, stubby bill and a very long, narrow tail. The sexes look the same and young birds undergo a complete moult to adult plumage before the first winter. The plumage is mainly black and white, with variable amounts of grey and pink.


Voice
This species is often identified in the field through hearing its distinctive vocalisations. When they are in flocks, they issue constant contact calls, so they are often heard before they are seen. They have three main calls, a single high pitched pit, a 'triple trill' eez-eez-eez, and a rattling schnuur. These calls become faster and louder when the birds cross open ground or if an individual becomes separated from the group.


Distribution and habitat
The long-tailed tit is widespread throughout temperate Northern Europe and the Palearctic region, extending into the boreal Scandinavia and south into the Mediterranean zone. It inhabits deciduous and mixed woodlands with a well-developed shrub layer and favours edge habitats. It can also be found in scrubland, heathland with scattered trees, bushes and hedges, farmland, riverine woodland, and parks and gardens. The bird's year-round diet of insects and its social foraging behaviour influence its choice of habitat in winter, which is typically deciduous woodland with a canopy of oak, ash and locally sycamore species. For nesting, a strong preference is shown for scrub areas. The nest is often built in thorny bushes less than 3 metres (10 feet) above the ground.


Behaviour and ecology


Food and feeding
The long-tailed tit is insectivorous throughout the year. Its diet consists predominantly of arthropods, with a preference for the eggs and larvae of moths and butterflies. It occasionally takes vegetable matter such as seeds  in autumn and winter.


Nest
The nest of the long-tailed tit is constructed from four materials: lichen, feathers, spider egg cocoons, spider silk, and moss, with over 6,000 pieces used for a typical nest. The nest is a flexible sac with a small, round entrance at the top, suspended either low in a gorse or bramble bush or high up in the forks of tree branches. The structural stability of the nest is provided by a mesh of moss and spider silk; the tiny leaves of the moss act as hooks and the spider's silken thread provides the loops, thus producing a natural form of velcro. The tit lines the outside with hundreds of flakes of pale lichens to provide camouflage. The inside of the nest is lined with more than 2,000 downy feathers to provide insulation. The nests suffer a high rate of predation, with only 17% success.


Social behaviour

Outside of the breeding season, long-tailed tits form compact flocks of six to seventeen birds. These flocks are composed of family parties (parents and offspring) from the previous breeding season, along with any additional adults who helped raise a brood. These flocks will occupy and defend territories against neighbouring flocks. The driving force behind the flocking behaviour is thought to be that of winter roosting, being susceptible to cold; huddling increases survival through cold nights. When the breeding season begins, the flocks break up, and the birds attempt to breed in monogamous pairs. Males remain within the winter territory, while females have a tendency to wander to neighbouring territories.
Since long-tailed tits are cooperative breeders, where opposite sex adult relatives are spatially clustered, they risk inbreeding fitness costs.  However, such inbreeding fitness costs are avoided by active kin discrimination during mate choice.  Kin discrimination appears to be based on distinguishing between the vocal calls of close kin and nonkin.
Pairs whose nests fail have three options: they can try again, abandon nesting for the season, or help at a neighbouring nest. Studies have shown that failed pairs tend to split up and assist with the nest duties of male relatives, recognition being established vocally. The helped nests have greater success due to higher provisioning rates and better nest defence. At the end of the breeding season, in June and July, the birds reform the winter flocks in their winter territory.


Helpers
There is a high nest failure rate due to high predation. If a nest fails after the beginning of May, the failed breeders will not attempt to re-nest, but they may become helpers at the nest of another pair, who are usually related. One study found that around 50% of nests had one or more helpers. By helping close relatives, helpers indirectly benefit in terms of fitness by increasing the survivability of related offspring. They may also gain greater access to mates and territories in the future. Helpers also gain experience in raising young, meaning that their future offspring have a better chance of survival.
Both males and females are equally likely to become helpers. Parents may supplement their own efforts with the help of others, or, at the other extreme, reduce their own efforts in favour of the helpers' efforts. Although the survival rate for adults of both sexes is the same, juvenile males have a higher survival rate than juvenile females. Offspring raised with helpers have a higher survival rate than those raised without. Failed breeders who became helpers had a higher survival rate than those who did not. This may be due to the reduced energy expenditure involved in sharing a nest. This is similar phenomenon to that observed in acorn woodpeckers and green wood hoopoes. However, failed breeders that did not help are more likely to breed successfully in subsequent years, so there may be a cost of helping. This may be due to helpers having relatively poorer body conditions at the end of the breeding season, similar to pied kingfisher and white-winged chough. Successful breeders have a survivability rate around the survivability of failed breeders who became helpers.


Status and conservation
Globally, the species is common throughout its range, only becoming scarce at the edge of the distribution. The IUCN, BirdLife International and The British Trust for Ornithology (BTO) all list the long-tailed tit as a 'species of least concern', currently under little or no threat and reasonably abundant.
Due to their small size they are vulnerable to extreme cold weather, with population losses of up to 80% being recorded in times of prolonged cold. It is thought that populations rapidly return to previous levels due to high breeding potential.


Gallery


Citations


General and cited references


External links

Xeno-canto: audio recordings of the long-tailed tit
Long-tailed tit videos, photos & sounds on the Internet Bird Collection
Calls of several long-tailed tits—a British Library sound recording.
"Ageing and sexing" (PDF; 1.48 MB) by Javier Blasco-Zumeta & Gerd-Michael Heinze

The '''long-tailed tit''' or '''long-tailed bushtit''' ('''''Aegithalos caudatus''''') is a common [[bird]] species belonging to the [[bushtit]] family found throughout [[Eurasia]]. An [[insectivore]], it inhabits [[deciduous]] and mixed woodlands as well as [[Shrubland|scrub]], [[heathland]], farmland, parks and gardens. The plumage of this small long-tailed bird is predominantly black and white with varying amounts of pink and grey. Northern subspecies are paler and have completely white heads, lacking the large dark eyebrows of southern populations. It is a [[sociality|social]] bird that forms compact family flocks of six to seventeen individuals outside of the [[breeding season]], when these flocks break up. It has a strong preference for nesting in scrub areas and often builds its nest in thorny bushes less than {{convert|3|m|ft|0|abbr=off}} above the ground.

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The long-tailed tit was [[Species description|formally described]] by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in 1758 in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' under the [[binomial name]] ''Parus caudatus''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=C. | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis, Volume 1| volume=1 | edition=10th | page=190 | publisher=Holmiae:Laurentii Salvii | language=la | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727099 }}</ref> The current genus name ''[[Aegithalos]]'' was a term used by [[Aristotle]] for some European tits, including the long-tailed tit. The [[specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]] ''caudatus'' is the [[Latin]] word for "tailed".<ref name=job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A | year= 2010 | title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher= Christopher Helm | location = London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=[https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n94 94]}}</ref> Linnaeus did not invent this Latin name. "''Parus caudatus''" had been used by earlier authors such as the Swiss naturalist [[Conrad Gessner]] in 1555,<ref>{{cite book| last=Gesner | first=Conrad | author-link=Conrad Gessner | title=Historiae animalium liber III qui est de avium natura. Adiecti sunt ab initio indices alphabetici decem super nominibus avium in totidem linguis diversis: & ante illos enumeratio avium eo ordine quo in hoc volumine continentur | year=1555 | publisher= Froschauer | location=Zurich | language=la | url=https://gdz.sub.uni-goettingen.de/id/PPN472755714?tify={%22pages%22:[651],%22view%22:%22info%22} | page=617}}</ref> the Italian naturalist [[Ulisse Aldrovandi]] in 1599,<ref>{{ cite book | last=Aldrovandi| first=Ulisse | author-link=Ulisse Aldrovandi | year=1637 | title=Ulyssis Aldrovandi philosophi ac medici Bononiensis historiam naturalem in gymnasio Bononiensi profitentis, Ornithologiae | volume=2 | language=la | place=Bononiae (Bologna, Italy) | publisher=Franciscum de Franciscis Senensem | pages=715–720 Lib. 17 Cap. 15 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/53872347 }}</ref> and the English ornithologist [[Francis Willughby]] in 1676. Willughby listed the English name as the "long tail'd titmouse".<ref>{{ cite book | last=Willughby | first=Francis | author-link=Francis Willughby | year=1676 | title=Ornithologiae libri tres | language=la | place=London | publisher=John Martyn | page=176 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=EH2YLUUY57oC&pg=PA176 }}</ref> Its previous common nickname in everyday English was the bum-towel, from the shape of its tail.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Thomas|first=Keith Vivian|title=Man and the natural world: changing attitudes in England, 1500-1800|publisher=Allen Lane|year=1991|isbn=978-0-14-193604-8|location=London|pages=|oclc=644450864}}</ref>

The long-tailed tit was originally classified as a true tit of the ''[[Parus]]'' genus. Since then, it and its relatives have  been reclassified as a distinct family, the [[Aegithalidae]], containing three [[genera]]:

* ''Aegithalos'' (long-tailed tits), nine living species including ''A. caudatus''
* ''Psaltria'' ([[pygmy bushtit]]), [[monotypic]]
* ''Psaltriparus'' ([[American bushtit]]), monotypic.
* ''Aegithalos caudatus caudatus × europaeus'' ([[Northern × Central European Long-tailed Tit]])<ref>https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1585309-Aegithalos-caudatus-caudatus---europaeus</ref>


This species is the only representative of the family Aegithalidae found in northern [[Eurasia]].{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=132}} The long-tailed tit exhibits complex global variation with 17 races recognised,<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2017 | title=Bushtits, leaf warblers, reed warblers | work=World Bird List | version=v8.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/bushtits/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=21 March 2018 }}</ref> divisible into three groups:<ref name="hq1996">{{cite book |last1=Harrap |first1=S. |last2=Quinn |first2=D. |title=Tits, Nuthatches & Treecreepers |publisher=Christopher Helm  |series=Helm Identification Guides |date=1996 |isbn=0-7136-3964-4 |oclc=943928899}}</ref>

[[File:Schwanzmeise (Aegithalos caudatus) distribution map.png|300px|alt=Map of subspecies distribution for long-tailed tits|thumb|left|Distribution map of long-tailed tits]]
[[File:Parks.Range.Aegithalos.caudatus.caudatus.jpg|thumb|''Aegithalos caudatus caudatus'' with white head in Berlin]]
[[File:Long-tailed_tit_in_Russia_in_the_forest.jpg|thumb|356x356px|Long-tailed tit in Russia]]
* the ''A. c. caudatus'' group in northern Europe and Asia. ''A. c. caudatus'' has a pure white head[[File:Long-tailed_tit_on_the_tree.jpg|left|thumb|433x433px]]
* the ''A. c. europaeus'' group in southern and western Europe, north-east [[China]], and [[Japan]]. Separating ''A. c. rosaceus'' from other members of the ''A. c. europaeus'' group though is problematic, relying on varying thickness of the crown stripes and amount of streaks and colour on the underparts<ref name="hq1996" />
* the ''A. c. alpinus'' group in [[Mediterranean]] Europe and south-west Asia.

The [[silver-throated bushtit]] (''Aegithalos glaucogularis'') from eastern China was formerly considered [[conspecific]] with the long-tailed tit, but was later split due to significant plumage and genetic differences.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Harrap | first=S | year=2018 | title=Silver-throated Tit (''Aegithalos glaucogularis'') | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | journal=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher=Lynx Edicions | doi=10.2173/bow.lottit5.02 | url=https://www.hbw.com/node/59742 | access-date=24 March 2018 |url-access=subscription  }}</ref><ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Päckert | first1=M. | last2=Martens | first2=J. | last3=Sun| first3=Y.-H. | year=2010 | title=Phylogeny of long-tailed tits and allies inferred from mitochondrial and nuclear markers (Aves: Passeriformes, Aegithalidae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=55 | issue=3 | pages=952–967 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2010.01.024 | pmid=20102744 | bibcode=2010MolPE..55..952P }}</ref>

Where the groups meet, there are extensive areas occupied by highly variable 'hybrids'. The British long-tailed tit, subspecies ''A. c. rosaceus'', belongs to the ''A. c. europaeus'' group.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=133}}

==Description==
This species has been described as a tiny (at only {{cvt|13|-|15|cm|in|0}} in length, including its {{cvt|7|-|9|cm|in|frac=2}} tail), round-bodied [[tit (bird)|tit]] with a short, stubby [[Beak|bill]] and a very long, narrow tail.<ref name="hq1996"/> The sexes look the same and young birds undergo a complete [[moult]] to adult plumage before the first winter. The plumage is mainly black and white, with variable amounts of grey and pink.<ref name="hq1996"/>

===Voice===
This species is often identified in the field through hearing its distinctive vocalisations. When they are in flocks, they issue constant contact calls, so they are often heard before they are seen. They have three main calls, a single high pitched ''pit'', a 'triple trill' ''eez-eez-eez'', and a rattling ''schnuur''. These calls become faster and louder when the birds cross open ground or if an individual becomes separated from the group.<ref name=gaston73/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The long-tailed tit is widespread throughout temperate Northern Europe and the Palearctic region, extending into the [[boreal ecosystem|boreal]] [[Scandinavia]] and south into the Mediterranean zone.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=134}} It inhabits [[deciduous]] and mixed woodlands with a well-developed shrub layer and favours edge habitats. It can also be found in scrubland, [[heathland]] with scattered trees, bushes and hedges, farmland, [[riverine]] woodland,<ref name="hq1996" /> and parks and gardens. The bird's year-round diet of insects and its social [[foraging]] behaviour influence its choice of habitat in winter, which is typically deciduous woodland with a canopy of [[Quercus|oak]], [[Fraxinus|ash]] and locally [[Acer (genus)|sycamore]] species. For nesting, a strong preference is shown for scrub areas.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=134}} The nest is often built in thorny bushes less than {{convert|3|m|ft|0|abbr=off}} above the ground.<ref name="hq1996" />

==Behaviour and ecology==
===Food and feeding===
The long-tailed tit is [[insectivorous]] throughout the year. Its diet consists predominantly of [[arthropods]], with a preference for the eggs and larvae of [[Lepidoptera|moths and butterflies]]. It occasionally takes vegetable matter such as seeds  in autumn and winter.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Aegithalos_caudatus/ | title=Aegithalos caudatus (Long-tailed tit) | website=[[Animal Diversity Web]] }}</ref>{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=136}}

===Nest===
The nest of the long-tailed tit is constructed from four materials: [[lichen]], [[feather]]s, [[spider silk|spider egg cocoons]], [[spider silk]], and [[moss]], with over 6,000 pieces used for a typical nest<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Long-tailed Tit Nesting: A Complete Guide |url=https://www.birdfact.com/articles/long-tailed-tit-nesting |access-date=2026-03-12 |website=www.birdfact.com |language=en}}</ref>. The nest is a flexible sac with a small, round entrance at the top, suspended either low in a [[gorse]] or [[bramble]] bush or high up in the forks of tree branches. The structural stability of the nest is provided by a mesh of moss and spider silk; the tiny leaves of the moss act as hooks and the spider's silken thread provides the loops, thus producing a natural form of [[velcro]].<ref name="Hansell2007">{{cite book|last=Hansell|first=Michael Henry|title=Built by animals: the natural history of animal architecture |url=https://archive.org/details/builtbyanimalsna00hans|url-access=registration|year=2007|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-920556-1|pages=[https://archive.org/details/builtbyanimalsna00hans/page/76 76], 77}}</ref> The tit lines the outside with hundreds of flakes of pale lichens to provide camouflage. The inside of the nest is lined with more than 2,000 downy feathers to provide insulation.<ref name="Hansell2007"/> The nests suffer a high rate of predation, with only 17% success.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Hatchwell | first1=B.J. | last2=Russell | first2=A.F. | last3=Fowlie | first3=M.K. | last4=Ross | first4=D.J. | year=1999 | title=Reproductive success and nest-site selection in a cooperative breeder: effect of experience and a direct benefit of helping | journal=Auk | volume=116 | issue=2 | pages=355–363 | url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v116n02/p0355-p0363.pdf | doi=10.2307/4089370| jstor=4089370 }}</ref>

<gallery widths="200px" heights="180px">
File:Long-tailed Tit nest building Dungeness, Kent 07 Mar 2009 A.jpg|A long-tailed tit in its nest
File:Aegithalos caudatus MWNH 2283.JPG|Egg, Collection [[Museum Wiesbaden]]
Aegithalos caudatus  MHNT.ZOO.2010.11.180.1.jpg |''Aegithalos caudatus '' – [[MHNT]]
</gallery>

===Social behaviour===
[[File:Territory in bird life p145 (cropped).png|thumb| "Males fighting for the possession of territory. The feathers have been torn from the crown of the defeated and dying rival" ([[Henry Eliot Howard|H. E. Howard]] (1920), ''Territory in Bird Life'', p. 145)]]
[[File:Aegithalos caudatus - kanagawa - 2026 1 26-1080p30.webm|thumb|Long-tailed tits flying in the trees in [[Japan]], 2026]]
Outside of the breeding season, long-tailed tits form compact flocks of six to seventeen birds. These flocks are composed of family parties (parents and offspring) from the previous breeding season, along with any additional adults who helped raise a brood.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=138}} These flocks will occupy and defend territories against neighbouring flocks.<ref name=gaston73>{{ cite journal | last=Gaston | first=A.J. | year=1973 | title=The ecology and behaviour of the long-tailed tit | journal=Ibis | volume=115 | issue=3 | pages=330–351 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1973.tb01974.x}}</ref> The driving force behind the flocking behaviour is thought to be that of winter [[roosting]], being susceptible to cold; huddling increases survival through cold nights.<ref name="gp1988">{{ cite journal | last1=Glen | first1=N.W. | last2=Perrins | first2=C.M. | year=1988 | title=Cooperative breeding by long-tailed tits | journal=British Birds | volume=81 | issue=12 | pages=630–641 | url=https://britishbirds.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/article_files/V81/V81_N12/V81_N12_P630_641_A154.pdf }}</ref> When the breeding season begins, the flocks break up, and the birds attempt to breed in monogamous pairs.<ref name=mcgowan>{{cite journal|last1=McGowan | first1=A. | last2=Hatchwell | first2=B.J. | last3=Woodburn | first3=R.J.W. |  year=2003 | title=The effect of helping behaviour on the survival of juvenile and adult long-tailed tits ''Aegithalos caudatus'' |journal=Journal of Animal Ecology | volume=72 | issue=3| pages=491–9 | doi=10.1046/j.1365-2656.2003.00719.x | doi-access=free| bibcode=2003JAnEc..72..491M }}</ref> Males remain within the winter territory, while females have a tendency to wander to neighbouring territories.<ref name=gaston73/>

Since long-tailed tits are cooperative breeders, where opposite sex adult relatives are spatially clustered, they risk [[inbreeding depression|inbreeding fitness costs]].<ref name = Leedale2020>{{cite journal |vauthors=Leedale AE, Simeoni M, Sharp SP, Green JP, Slate J, Lachlan RF, Robinson EJ, Hatchwell BJ |title=Cost, risk, and avoidance of inbreeding in a cooperatively breeding bird |journal=Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A |volume=117 |issue=27 |pages=15724–30 |date=July 2020 |pmid=32571952 |pmc=7355050 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1918726117 |doi-access=free |bibcode=2020PNAS..11715724L }}</ref>  However, such inbreeding fitness costs are avoided by active kin discrimination during mate choice.<ref name = Leedale2020/>  Kin discrimination appears to be based on distinguishing between the vocal calls of close kin and nonkin.<ref name = Leedale2020/>

Pairs whose nests fail have three options: they can try again, abandon nesting for the season, or help at a neighbouring nest. Studies have shown that failed pairs tend to split up and assist with the nest duties of male relatives,<ref name="gp1988"/><ref name="h2001">{{ cite journal | last1=Hatchwell | first1=J. | last2=Ross | first2=D.J. | last3=Fowlie | first3=M.K. | last4=McGowan | first4=A. | year=2001 | title=Kin discrimination in cooperatively breeding long-tailed tits | journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B | volume=268 | issue=1470 | pages=885–890 | doi=10.1098/rspb.2001.1598 | pmc=1088684 | pmid=11370960}}</ref><ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Sharp | first1=S.P. | last2=Simeoni | first2=M. | last3=Hatchwell | first3= B. | year=2008 | title=Dispersal of sibling coalitions promotes helping among immigrants in a cooperatively breeding bird | journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences | volume=275| issue=1647 | pages=2125–30 | doi=10.1098/rspb.2008.0398 | doi-access=free | pmc=2603207 | pmid=18522914}}</ref> recognition being established vocally.<ref name="h2001"/> The helped nests have greater success due to higher provisioning rates and better nest defence.<ref name="gp1988"/> At the end of the breeding season, in June and July, the birds reform the winter flocks in their winter territory.<ref name="gaston73"/>

===Helpers===
There is a high nest failure rate due to high predation.<ref name=":0" /> If a nest fails after the beginning of May, the failed breeders will not attempt to re-nest, but they may become helpers at the nest of another pair, who are usually related. One study found that around 50% of nests had one or more helpers. By helping close relatives, helpers indirectly benefit in terms of fitness by increasing the survivability of related offspring. They may also gain greater access to mates and territories in the future. Helpers also gain experience in raising young, meaning that their future offspring have a better chance of survival.

Both males and females are equally likely to become helpers. Parents may supplement their own efforts with the help of others, or, at the other extreme, reduce their own efforts in favour of the helpers' efforts. Although the survival rate for adults of both sexes is the same, juvenile males have a higher survival rate than juvenile females. Offspring raised with helpers have a higher survival rate than those raised without. Failed breeders who became helpers had a higher survival rate than those who did not. This may be due to the reduced energy expenditure involved in sharing a nest. This is similar phenomenon to that observed in [[acorn woodpecker]]s and [[green wood hoopoe]]s. However, failed breeders that did not help are more likely to breed successfully in subsequent years, so there may be a cost of helping. This may be due to helpers having relatively poorer body conditions at the end of the breeding season, similar to [[pied kingfisher]] and [[white-winged chough]]. Successful breeders have a survivability rate around the survivability of failed breeders who became helpers.<ref name=mcgowan/>

==Status and conservation==
Globally, the species is common throughout its range, only becoming scarce at the edge of the distribution.<ref name="hq1996"/> The [[IUCN]], [[BirdLife International]] and The [[British Trust for Ornithology]] (BTO) all list the long-tailed tit as a 'species of [[least concern]]', currently under little or no threat and reasonably abundant.<ref name="iucn" /><ref>{{ Cite book | last=Robinson | first=R.A. | title=BirdFacts: Long-tailed Tit ''Aegithalos caudatus'' | year=1967 | volume=12 | publisher=British Trust for Ornithology | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482170 | access-date=30 March 2018 }}</ref>

Due to their small size they are vulnerable to extreme cold weather, with population losses of up to 80% being recorded in times of prolonged cold. It is thought that populations rapidly return to previous levels due to high breeding potential.<ref name="hq1996"/>

zh.extract:
北长尾山雀（学名：Aegithalos caudatus）为长尾山雀科长尾山雀属的鸟类。分布于自北欧、东北欧、东经西伯利亚、至勘察加半岛、萨哈林岛、日本、蒙古、朝鲜以及中国的新疆和东北地区等地，多见于山地针叶林或针阔混交林、在辽宁尤以东部山区的落叶松林较常见以及冬季或迁平原。该物种的模式产地在瑞典。而北長尾山雀的巢一般是由四種材料如地衣、羽毛、蜘蛛蛋繭和苔蘚所製成。
北长尾山雀体重约8.6克，翼长约63.1毫米，嘴峰长约7.6毫米，喙宽度约2.5毫米，喙厚度约3.3毫米，跗蹠骨长约15.9毫米，尾长约87.8毫米。北长尾山雀在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的高大树木组成的森林中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是陆生无脊椎动物。


亚种
指名亚种 A. c. caudatus：分布于斯堪的纳维亚半岛和欧洲东北部至西伯利亚、中国东北、朝鲜半岛和日本。
europaeus Group
A. c. rosaceus：分布于不列颠群岛。
A. c. aremoricus：分布于法国西部、海峡群岛和约岛。
A. c. taiti：分布于法国南部和西南部至西班牙和葡萄牙西北部。
A. c. europaeus：分布于法国东北部和德国至意大利北部、罗马尼亚西部和保加利亚北部。
A. c. macedonicus：分布于巴尔干半岛。
A. c. tauricus：分布于克里米亚半岛南部。
trivirgatus group
A. c. magnus：分布于朝鲜半岛中南部和对马岛。
A. c. trivirgatus：分布于日本本州岛、粟島、佐渡岛和沖島及韩国濟州島。
A. c. kiusiuensis：分布于日本四国岛、九州岛和屋久岛。
alpinus Group
A. c. irbii：分布于西班牙南部、葡萄牙和科西嘉岛。
A. c. italiae：分布于意大利的大陆地区和斯洛文尼亚西南部。
A. c. siculus：分布于西西里岛。
A. c. tephronotus：分布于小亚细亚。
A. c. major：分布于高加索至外高加索西部和中部地区。
A. c. alpinus：分布于阿塞拜疆东南部至伊朗北部和土库曼斯坦西南部。
A. c. passekii：分布于伊朗西南部。


保護狀態
在全球範圍內，該物種在其整個範圍內都很常見，但是在分佈範圍的邊緣變得比較稀缺。IUCN、國際鳥盟和英國鳥類學信託基金會都將長尾山雀列為無危物種，因為目前它們受到的威脅很小或沒有威脅，而且數量相當多。由於它們的體積小，所以它們很容易遭受極端寒冷的天氣，在長時間的寒冷時期，族群的數量可能會損失高達80％。但是人們認為，由於高繁殖力的關係，種群將會迅速恢復到以前的水平。
